What This Is

This is a list of items and evidence we should get for every case at the intake stage, and case setup phase if we accept the case.

  1. Copy of initial police report (if you have obtained it)
  2. Incident report if a slip and fall or non-auto case
  3. Hospital ER records or discharge forms
  4. List of the doctors now treating for the injuries, or their business cards
  5. Last several pay stubs prior to collision/incident
  6. Worker's compensation claim information (if injured while at work) and copies of all the forms completed.
  7. Driver's License
  8. Social Security Card
  9. Automobile insurance card (and/or the automobile policy)
  10. Health insurance card(s), Medicare Card(s), Medicaid Card(s), etc.
  11. Bills received to date for treatment of the injuries
  12. Any written statement client gave to any insurance adjuster following the collision
  13. Photographs of injuries, in the hospital room, ambulating on crutches, etc.
  14. Photographs and videos of the scene where the collision occurred
  15. Names of any witnesses at the scene
  16. Employer's name, address, and the name of the personnel manager (if known)
  17. Prescription bottles for all medications taken before the collision and any new medications prescribed since the collision
  18. If this is a wrongful death claim, bring copies of the death certificate, a copy of the Last Will & Testament, any Letters of Administration listing who the Personal Representative of the Estate is, and the name and address of the probate attorney.

Trial Binder. Begin 6 months before the trial date.

0. COVER PAGE – TABLE OF CONTENTS

1. CASE SUMMARY-MEMO

2. POLICE REPORT

3. PROPERTY DAMAGE PHOTOS

4. BEFORE AND AFTER VISUALS

  • Family Photos/videos before and after
  • Pre/Post videos of activity (videos of home exercises and/or therapy and/or surgery or injections). Photos of prescription bottles. Photographs of the front offices of medical and physical therapy offices and hospitals to put into visuals for use at trial.

5. MED SUMMARY/TIMELINE

6. MEDICAL HOT DOCS

  • The specific page of medical records for each GOOD fact important to your damages, highlighted
  • The specific page of medical records for each BAD fact important to your damages, highlighted

7. DAMAGING HOT DOCS

  • Pull every document that is bad for you, that defense will use, lump them all together

8. THE HUMAN STORY

  • Steve Halteman style history of PL and family
  • Human story highlights
  • Potential problem areas

9. MINI OPENING

  • Draft of Plaintiff's mini-opening. Every problem area you intend to address in voir dire should be in the mini-opening in one form or another.
  • Also anticipate and write the defense mini opening

10. LIST OF VOIR DIRE TOPICS TO BE ADDRESSED

  • First page, BRUTAL HONESTY script reminder
  • Second page, list of topics to be addressed
  • Also, pull any specific jury instructions you'd like to use during voir dire

11. OPENING STATEMENT

  • PowerPoint/Keynote slides, demonstratives, jury instructions

12. WITNESS BY WITNESS TABS

  • Name of witness and brief description of who they are (e.g. John Doe, Plaintiff's husband; Dr. Humphrey, ER Doc who read CT)
  • Short list of facts you need from that witness
  • Copy of each page of any exhibit you intend to use/introduce with that witness
  • List of trouble spots for cross
  • The idea is, if you did not know the case well, you could turn to the tab, see what you needed and could get it.

13. CLOSING CHECKLIST

  • All facts needed (with check box next to them, when your witness gives you the fact, check it off)
  • All exhibits admitted (same with check boxes)
  • This should be like a road map to help write the examinations and develop the lists of facts/exhibits from witnesses. By end of trial there should be a checkmark next to each fact you proved and each exhibit you admitted.
  • Basic architecture of closing Keynote/PowerPoint

14. DAMAGES — DON'T ASK, TELL

  • We are not asking for money/award/verdict. We are telling them what the facts and the law have shown to be the fair value.
  • "We believe that a jury verdict of $$$$ will be justified by the evidence and the law." What is your number? How do you get there?

15. DEMONSTRATIVE EXHIBITS

  • Videos on the internet that explain the injury can be turned into presentations with expert sign-off that it's demonstrative.

16. POCKET BRIEFS

  • Re: Numbers in Voir Dire and Opening
  • Re: No more Settlement Conferences
  • Re: Experts and Hearsay
  • Re: Excluding Sub Rosa

17. TO DO

  • Experts to still be retained
  • Medical Follow Ups for Plaintiff before trial and/or expert depo
  • Additional Discovery to be obtained
  • Focus Group plans
  • Misc to do lists

Watch your tone: imagine every letter you write on an exhibit board and carried into the courthouse.

Tip: Demonizing the defendant or the insurance company makes no sense. The letters we write should increase the likelihood of cooperation and resolution, not decrease it. The tone should be clear and firm but not strident.

GOAL: motivate and assist the carrier to pay our client full market value. Start by documenting every aspect of your claim. Write soon and often to the carrier. Setting the reserves high and early helps them to help you. This makes it a shorter step for them to later write you a large check.

  • Be respectful
  • Imagine a judge or jury paying judgment on you based on what you wrote in the letter.
  • If you aren't certain that the language is in your client's best interest, don't write it.
  • Avoid hyperbole: clearly, extremely, obviously
  • Avoid inflammatory remarks: absurd, blatant, egregious
  • Never name call
  • Never lie, fabricate facts, or misrepresent legal authority.

Purpose: To establish a protocol for handling situations where a client reinjures a body part that is already part of their personal injury claim, ensuring proper documentation and medical support to maintain the viability of the case.

Legal Foundation: "When a person is injured by the negligence of another and, despite the exercise of ordinary and reasonable diligence in the treatment of the injuries, the individual is involved in another accident because of the injuries sustained in the first accident, the tortfeasor in the first accident is also responsible for the subsequent injuries." — Daliendo v Johnson, 1989, 2nd Dept.

Step 1: Immediate Client Communication

  1. Upon learning of a possible reinjury, a staff member or attorney must contact the client immediately.
  2. Confirm whether the reinjury affects the same body part as the original claim.
  3. If it is the same body part, schedule a detailed discussion with the client as soon as possible.

Step 2: Educating the Client

  1. Explain the law, emphasizing that if the reinjury is a direct consequence of the original injury, it can still be included in their case.
  2. Clarify that for this to be valid, the reinjury must be properly documented and supported by medical evidence.
  3. Inform the client they must consult with their doctor immediately to ensure medical documentation reflects the aggravation of their condition.

Step 3: Medical Documentation & Proof

  1. Advise the client to make an appointment with their treating physician as soon as possible.
  2. Request the doctor document the reinjury and how it relates to the original injury, and any worsening of symptoms or additional treatments required.
  3. If necessary, consult with a medical expert to further substantiate the reinjury claim.

Step 4: Case Strategy & Insurance Communication

  1. Assess the impact of the reinjury on the overall claim.
  2. If necessary, adjust the legal strategy and damages calculation to reflect the aggravated injury.
  3. Notify the insurance company or opposing counsel, if appropriate, with updated medical documentation.
  4. Prepare for potential disputes regarding causation.

Step 5: Ongoing Client Monitoring & Case Updates

  1. Maintain regular follow-up with the client to track medical treatment and progress.
  2. Continue gathering additional evidence needed to reinforce the claim.
  3. Keep the client informed about the impact of the reinjury on settlement negotiations or litigation strategy.

Compliance & Documentation: Ensure all communications, medical records, and legal updates are documented in the case management system. Maintain a checklist to confirm all required steps have been completed.

Rule

Under 11 NYCRR 65-3.12(a)(9), when a person is injured while occupying a bus not principally used for personal purposes, that person must submit their no-fault claim to the insurer of a household automobile policy, rather than the insurer of the bus.

"Where a person is injured while occupying a bus other than one used principally for personal purposes, and such person is not otherwise covered under a policy of insurance issued to the owner or operator of such bus, the first-party benefits shall be payable by the insurer of such person's own policy or the policy of a relative residing in the same household."

Analysis

This provision creates an exception to the general rule that no-fault benefits are payable by the insurer of the vehicle the injured party occupied. In the case of school buses, which are commercial vehicles used for transporting students, this regulation shifts primary no-fault responsibility to the injured person's own household policy.

Therefore, if a child is injured while riding a school bus and resides in a household with a valid auto policy providing no-fault coverage, that household insurer is the primary payer of PIP benefits, not the school bus company's insurer.

Conclusion

Pursuant to 11 NYCRR 65-3.12(a)(9), the proper no-fault insurer is the child's household auto insurer when the injury occurs on a school bus not used principally for personal purposes. Claims should be submitted to the household policy first.

Department: Litigation | Effective Date: April 25, 2025 | Prepared by: Buffalo Injury Law Firm | Version: 1.0

Objective

To establish a uniform procedure for scheduling depositions in personal injury cases, ensuring the attorney has sufficient preparation time and that deposition logistics are handled efficiently.

Policy

Depositions shall only be scheduled:

  • On Tuesdays, Wednesdays, or Thursdays
  • Four (4) weeks or more in advance (do not schedule within 4 weeks)
  • Only after 45 days have passed since HIPAA authorizations were served to the defense

Procedure

Step 1: Track HIPAA Authorization Date — Record the service date in the case calendar and case management system. Set a follow-up reminder for 45 days after service to initiate deposition scheduling.

Step 2: Check Attorney Availability — After the 45-day period, identify three available deposition dates (Tue/Wed/Thu, at least 4 weeks out). Confirm no conflicting obligations.

Step 3: Email Opposing Counsel — Draft an email proposing the three dates, attach the original plaintiff's deposition notice, CC the attorney.

Sample: "I'm writing to coordinate deposition scheduling in the above-referenced matter. Please let me know your availability for one of the following proposed dates: [Tue], [Wed], [Thu]. Please advise which date works best, or if none work, please provide three alternative dates."

Step 4: Confirm & Calendar — Once opposing counsel confirms, add to case calendar, schedule on Remote Legal, notify the client, confirm court reporter if needed.

Step 5: Begin Client Prep — Schedule a pre-deposition prep session at least one week before the deposition. Email the client confirmation, instructions, and FAQs.

Compliance Notes

Do not deviate from the Tuesday–Thursday rule unless authorized by the attorney. Always allow at least 4 weeks between scheduling and the deposition date. Log all communications in the case file.

items.d: What to do when a client has a concussion
items.content:
1. Intake Level - mTBI Red Flag Identification

Ask the client if they are experiencing any of: headache since incident, dizziness/balance problems, nausea/vomiting, confusion/fogginess, difficulty concentrating, short-term memory problems, blurred/double vision, light/noise sensitivity, fatigue/drowsiness, mood/irritability changes, sleep disturbances, loss of consciousness (even briefly), or a witness observed the client dazed/disoriented/slow to respond.

If ≥ 2 symptoms present or loss of consciousness/dazed state reported: flag case as "Suspected mTBI," notify attorney immediately, trigger mTBI workflow.

2. Initial mTBI Legal Checklist (Within 72 Hours)

  • Get detailed client narrative and symptoms
  • Photograph vehicle damage and scene (if applicable)
  • Request ambulance report and ER/hospital records
  • Get signed authorizations for MRI/CT records
  • Refer to neurologist or physiatrist
  • Refer to concussion clinic if available
  • Request DTI MRI if symptoms persist

3. Match Symptoms to Treatment

SymptomRecommended SpecialistRecommended Treatment
Memory loss / brain fogNeuropsychologistCognitive therapy, computerized cognitive training
Dizziness / balance issuesVestibular therapist (PT)Vestibular rehabilitation therapy (VRT)
Mood swings / anxiety / depressionNeuropsychologist / PsychiatristCBT, medication management
Light/noise sensitivityNeurologist / OTGraded exposure therapy, tinted glasses, sensory modulation
HeadachesNeurologist / Pain managementMeds, physical therapy, Botox (if chronic)
Vision problemsNeuro-optometristVision therapy
Sleep disturbancesSleep specialist / NeurologistSleep hygiene, behavioral therapy, melatonin trial

4. Ongoing Tracking Checklist (Weekly)

Monitor symptom progression, check if therapies have started, track missed appointments/barriers to care, document treatment updates weekly, monthly case review with attorney, update liability/damages assessment.

5. Evidence & Damages Documentation

Symptom timeline and progression; imaging results (CT, MRI, DTI); neuropsychological testing results; therapy notes (PT, cognitive, vestibular); impact statements from family/employer; long-term impairment ratings and wage loss evaluation.

items.d: Sending a letter of representation to the client's insurance.
items.content:
Our process for creating and emailing a letter of representation to our client's insurance company.

Why We Do This

The letter of representation notifies the carrier of the loss as required by the insurance contract, gives the adjuster an idea of the severity of our client's injuries so they can set adequate reserves, and gives us authority to speak to the insurance company.

How We Do This

Go to: Client > Insurance > Adjusters > Envelope Icon > LOR to Clt Ins

  2. Notify attorney in Slack that draft is ready.

items.t: Letter to clients about reporting symptoms
items.content:
Step 1: Draft Ltr to CL - symptom reporting

Step 2: Send email with template attached

Subject line: ⚠️ Important: How to Protect Your Case at Every Medical Appointment

Dear [Client First Name],

Your medical treatment is not only about healing — it's also the most important evidence in your injury case. What your doctors write in their notes becomes the official record that insurance companies, defense lawyers, and even juries rely on to understand your injuries.

Why This Matters: If something isn't mentioned in your records, it's as if it never happened. When you consistently report what you're feeling, it helps your doctors treat you properly and protects the truth about what you're going through.

What You Should Do: At every single medical visit, tell your doctors all your symptoms — big or small — even if they seem minor or unrelated. Describe everything: pain, stiffness, numbness, weakness, headaches, dizziness, sleep trouble, or any changes since your last visit. Example: "My neck pain is still there. I have headaches two or three times a week. My shoulder feels weaker, and I have trouble lifting laundry. Sleep is harder because of the pain." Do this with every provider you see, including your primary care doctor.

Avoid These Common Mistakes: Don't exaggerate or focus only on the worst pain. Avoid saying "I'm fine" or "It's okay" if you still have pain — that can make it look like your injuries resolved when they haven't. Be honest and complete; if you're improving, mention that too.

The Bottom Line: Every visit is an opportunity to tell your story — how you're healing, what still hurts, and how your injuries affect your daily life.

Warm regards, Your legal team — The Buffalo Injury Law Firm

Step 3: Send text reminder to read the email

Purpose

This SOP ensures that we use AI responsibly and ethically. Artificial Intelligence tools like ChatGPT are powerful for drafting, brainstorming, and research — but these systems are not HIPAA compliant, meaning anything entered into them is not guaranteed to be stored or transmitted securely under healthcare privacy standards. We must never share personally identifiable health information (PHI), but we can safely use AI on de-identified information.

Step 1: Understanding HIPAA and AI Parameters

ChatGPT and similar tools may process data on shared/external servers, and data entered could theoretically be used to train models — so these systems cannot guarantee HIPAA-compliant encryption or storage.

✅ You can discuss case facts, redacted medical findings, and general legal strategy.

❌ You cannot include personal identifiers or information that could trace back to a client (name, address, email, phone, DOB, SSN, claim number, identifying photos/videos).

Step 2: What You Can and Cannot Do

Category✅ Acceptable (De-Identified)🚫 Not Allowed (PHI/Identifiers)
Analyzing documents"Analyze this employment contract and scope of employment between driver and company.""Analyze these primary care records and tell me if they have any pre-existing complaints of neck pain."
Analyzing med rec excerptsUpload an excerpt reading "Herniated disc at C5-6 with nerve impingement" — no name/DOB/provider.Upload a full record showing name, DOB, provider info.
Crash details"Client was rear-ended by a commercial truck traveling 45 mph on a snowy road" — no names/plates.—
Summarize"Summarize phone conversation with defense attorney/adjuster and provide a client-friendly email update."—
Drafting legal work"Draft discovery objections for John Doe, Index No. 516792/25, against GEICO."—
Strategy discussions"I have a fall case against the City of Buffalo and we don't know if there is prior written notice — how should I investigate?"—
Drafting letters/emails/texts"Draft an email to opposing counsel stating their responses are overdue."—

Step 3: Practical Workflow — Safe AI Use

  1. Remove all identifiers — replace with placeholders like Client, Defendant, Insurer, Provider.
  2. Redact before uploading — copy only the relevant paragraph/section, confirm no identifiers remain.
  3. Work through drafts, then finalize internally — all final work product must be reviewed and approved by an attorney before saving/sending.
  4. Never upload complete medical records (embedded PHI and metadata).

Step 4: Review Before Submitting to Attorney

Confirm you have redacted/anonymized all PHI, reviewed the draft for accuracy and tone, and included your own summary/reasoning (don't just paste AI output).

Step 5: Approved AI Platforms and Accounts

Do not store client data in ChatGPT, Google Docs, or other shared cloud tools. Always access AI platforms from secure firm devices and connections.

Step 6: Firm Values and Mindset

AI is a thinking partner, not a substitute for human judgment. Our guiding principle: Protect client privacy and pursue truth with integrity.

"Whoever walks in integrity walks securely." — Proverbs 10:9

items.t: SOP: Endorsing Settlement Checks Under POA (NY)
items.content:
Task Information

Purpose: To establish a uniform, compliant process for endorsing settlement checks when the attorney is authorized to sign on behalf of a client pursuant to a valid Power of Attorney (POA), in accordance with New York ethics rules, banking requirements, and trust-account obligations.

When This Applies

Use this SOP only when: the check is payable to Client AND Law Firm, the attorney is signing for the client under a valid, revocable POA, and funds are being deposited into the Attorney Trust / IOLA Account.

Before You Sign (Required)

Confirm all of the following: signed POA is in the file; POA authorizes endorsement of settlement checks; client consent is in writing. If any item is missing → client must sign personally.

How to Endorse (Back of Check)

Client (via POA): [Client Name] by (attorney signature), [Attorney Name], Esq., as Attorney-in-Fact (POA)

Law Firm: The Buffalo Injury Law Firm, Attorney Signature

Rules

Signatures must be handwritten. Agency language is mandatory. Both payees must endorse. Deposit only into trust account.

Never Do This

❌ Sign only the client's name. ❌ Omit POA/agency language. ❌ Use verbal permission alone. ❌ Deposit into operating account.

Bright-Line Rule

No "by [Attorney], as Attorney-in-Fact" = defective endorsement.

items.d: How to send medical records requests.
items.content:
This is our process for requesting medical records from medical providers.

Why We Do This: Getting medical records is the biggest hurdle in evaluating our cases. It is time-consuming and requires lots of follow-up. Without records, we cannot prove the nature and extent of our clients' injuries.

How We Do This:

Prior Treatment

  1. Add all primary care providers
  2. Add all providers who have treated client for the same body part injured in our case
  3. Request records using ChartSquad (see CasePeer's ChartSquad integration article)
  4. Upload invoice to client's Invoice folder in OneDrive and add amount to client Cost tab

Current Treatment

  1. Go to Medical Treatment tab
  2. Find the provider whose records are needed
  3. Check notes section to confirm if it is a Preferred Provider
  4. If yes, generate template "Ltr to Provider - record request," email/fax it with the HIPAA authorization, update records status, and save confirmation in the client's record request folder
  5. If not a preferred provider, message the attorney to ask whether to send via ChartSquad or basic HIPAA request (usually depends on facility type and volume of records)

Follow-up

Once the request is sent, follow up with providers to stay top of mind — this is the best way to ensure timely delivery. Track requests via the tracking report or a reminder task. Goal: get records in less than two weeks.

What This Is

How we deposit money into a Qualified Settlement Fund (QSF). A QSF allows our clients to protect their government benefits and gives them time to figure out where to invest their settlement money.

How We Do This

  1. Add Eastern Point Trust Company to "Other Party" Tab
  2. Draft Ltr to QSF
  3. Make check payable to the name of the Settlement Fund, e.g. "The Buffalo Injury Law Firm and [Client Name]"
  4. Mail USPS First Class to: Eastern Point Trust Company, c/o Eastern Point – Settlement Processing, P.O. Box 232, Warrenton, VA 20188

IMPORTANT: To ensure proper and timely credit, email contributions@easternpointservices.com advising: that a check has been sent, the date on the check, the exact amount, the name of the Settlement Fund to be credited, and any tracking information.

For checks that must be sent to a physical address: 403 Holiday Court Suite 101, Warrenton, VA 20186.

items.d: Statutory requirements for filing a notice of claim.
items.content:
General Municipal Law § 50-e, Chapter 24, Article 4 (New York). See also Notice of Intention to File a Claim for claims against NY State.

1. When service required; time for service; upon whom service required

In any tort case where a notice of claim is required by law as a condition precedent to commencing an action against a public corporation (or an officer/employee thereof), the notice of claim must be served within 90 days after the claim arises — except in wrongful death actions, where the 90 days runs from the appointment of a representative of the decedent's estate. Service on an officer/employee individually is not itself a condition precedent unless the corporation has a statutory duty to indemnify.

2. Form of notice; contents

Must be in writing, sworn to by or on behalf of the claimant, and must set forth: (1) name and address of claimant and attorney; (2) nature of the claim; (3) time, place, and manner the claim arose; (4) items of damage/injury claimed. A supplemental claim must be provided within 15 days of request.

3. How served; when service by mail is complete

Personal delivery or registered/certified mail to the designated person, or to an attorney regularly representing the corporation. Service by mail is complete upon deposit in the post office. Defects in manner of service (but not timing) may still be valid under certain conditions (e.g., the corporation fails to return the notice specifying the defect within 30 days).

4. Requirements exclusive except as to conditions precedent for certain defects/snow or ice

No further notice or filing is required beyond this section, except for statutory notice requirements regarding defective/dangerous conditions of streets, highways, bridges, sidewalks, or snow/ice.

5. Application for leave to serve a late notice

The court may, in its discretion, extend the time to serve a notice of claim (not exceeding the statute of limitations), considering factors such as: whether the corporation/insurer had actual knowledge of the facts within the statutory period, infancy or incapacity of the claimant, justifiable reliance on settlement representations, excusable errors in identifying the correct public corporation, and prejudice to the corporation's defense.

6. Mistake, omission, irregularity, or defect

Good-faith mistakes not pertaining to manner/time of service may be corrected, supplied, or disregarded by the court if the other party is not prejudiced.

7. Applications under this section

Made to Supreme or County Court in the proper county; must be accompanied by the proposed notice of claim if seeking leave to serve late.

8. Inapplicability of section

Does not apply to workers' compensation, volunteer firefighter/ambulance worker benefit claims, or claims by infant wards against their own public corporation guardians. Also does not apply to certain claims arising from sexual offenses/incest against minors.

items.t: How to Enter New Case Data
items.content:
What This Is: How to open a new case once the retainer agreement is signed to ensure all necessary information is entered in the system for tracking and review.

Why We Do It This Way: Report. Track. Review. Reports based on key performance indicators are generated from case data — accurate data makes for accurate reports, and complete data lets anyone quickly understand and access a matter.

Brief the case with the attorney to establish an initial case plan (no cookie-cutter approach) — address injuries/treatment and investigation/coverage/responsible parties.

Once the case moves from intake to treating, the following MUST be entered (subheadings correspond to CasePeer subpages):

  • Case Index: Case type, date of incident, date of intake, date retained, status, source type/detail, firm file number (format YY-MMDD-Case#), name, gender, DOB, estimated case value, rating, high-value flag
  • Client Contact Details: Phone, address, email, key contact/spouse
  • Photos: Block email signature photos; save photos to the correct folder (e.g., vehicle photos → property damage folder)
  • Client Insurance: Plan type, claim #, limits, coverage status, liability status, property damage (always search dropdown before adding new entry)
  • Defendants: Add defendant(s) using police report contact info; add defendant insurance, plan type, claim #, limits, coverage/liability status, property damage
  • Incident: Details, description, incident report, DMV forms
  • Injuries: Current injuries, previous injuries, treatment details
  • Medical Treatment: Current providers, prior providers
  • Documents: Save new folder to OneDrive; copy subfolders into main folder
  • Employment: Add employer
  • Health Insurance: Add health insurance
  • Other Parties: Add witnesses
  • Attorney Liens: Add referring/prior attorney if applicable
  • Miscellaneous Liens: Add lienholder (Medicaid, Medicare, private health insurance, child support, etc.)
  • Costs: Add cost
  • Statute: Double-check statute date (default 3 years from incident; custom date needed for Notice of Claim)
  • Recordings: Upload videos if available

What This Is

Our process for getting treatment and well-being updates from our clients.

Why We Do It This Way

We must stay on top of clients' treatment and wellbeing — reasons people don't get appropriate treatment include work, family/kids, forgetfulness, being unsure which doctor to see, no transportation, fear, laziness, and the no-fault IME cut-off. Failing to get recommended treatment can ruin a case, primarily because without objective medical evidence of a serious injury, we lose. The defense will also argue the "gap in treatment" defense to break the temporal connection between the incident and the injury.

How We Do This

Text (usually preferred) or call the client to ask: How are you? With whom are you treating? How often? Any new providers planned? Any plans for injections? Have you had an MRI? Use discretion — ask what applies.

When We Do This

Check in every 2-3 weeks, with these benchmarks:

  • Month 1: Get the client into a medical doctor (ortho, PM&R, neurosurgeon) from the firm's provider list.
  • Month 3: Client should have established care (chiropractor/pain management + PT), treating several times a week, with MRIs/x-rays completed. Flag to attorney if not.
  • Month 6: Assess if pain/symptoms are improving. If plateaued, they need a specialist opinion (usually a surgeon). If no longer treating or feeling better, message attorney — case may be ready to settle.
  • Month 9: Should have all necessary tests and doctor visits to move forward with settlement/valuation. Begin preparing a Settlement Opportunity Letter.

Overview

Generally, case costs should not exceed 10% of the case value. Every case should have a budget set and reviewed by the attorney at the outset.

Non-approved expenses

  • Any expense $50.00 or less
  • Medical records that do not exceed $50.00
  • Police reports from DMV online
  • Driver abstract records

Pre-approval required

(Firm to define specific high-ticket categories requiring sign-off before purchase.)

Reporting guidelines

(Firm to document: how expenses get approved, who reports go to, what's needed for reimbursement, deadlines, and reimbursement turnaround time.)

items.d: Step-by-step process for handling no-fault claims, and deadlines.
items.content:
Our beginning-to-end operating procedures in handling the no-fault claim for car crash cases.

Setting it all up

Step 1: Get Claim Information

Ask client for their insurance card and claim information; call the client's insurer if the collision wasn't already reported. Need claim number, adjuster's name, phone, email, mailing address.

Step 2: Complete No-Fault Application

Under the client's tab, generate the No-Fault application from template and complete it fully (accident, treatment, injury, employment details). Schedule a phone call within 24-48 hours of signing the retainer to complete this.

Failure to submit this form within 30 days could result in a claim denial.

Step 3: Complete Letter of Representation (LOR)

Draft the LOR to client insurance once insurance info is entered into CasePeer. Review for completeness/errors. Notify the attorney once ready.

Step 4: Submit LOR and no-fault application

Email the adjuster the no-fault application and LOR with a delivery/read receipt requested.

Timing is of the essence: the no-fault application must be e-mailed within the first 30 days of the crash.

Step 5: Send No-Fault letters to client

Three letters emailed one day at a time starting the day after the retainer is signed, in this order: Ltr to Client re NF, Ltr to Client re NF wages, Ltr to Client re SIT.

Step 6: Confirmation

Confirm in writing with the no-fault adjuster that the LOR and application were received within 30 days; confirm coverage limits are listed in CasePeer.

Ongoing Maintenance

No-Fault Wages

No-fault pays wages for missed work due to injuries — if it doesn't, the client may be forced back to work prematurely, jeopardizing the case. After the client receives the NF wages letter, follow up with the PIP adjuster to confirm receipt of: NF-2 application, NF-6 wage verification (signed by employer), disability note from doctor, employer confirmation client isn't working. Self-employed/gig workers (DoorDash, Uber, etc.) additionally need: NF-7 self-employment wage verification, 6 months of transaction logs, 6 months of app earnings, 6 months of bank statements.

EVERY 30 DAYS, the client must submit a disability note to keep receiving wage benefits. Contact the client 7 days before the next pay period to remind them.

Insurance Medical Examinations

"Independent" medical examinations — appointments clients must attend with an insurance-selected doctor examining the claimed injuries.

Representation Complete

After the bodily injury case settles, reiterate to the client that we don't represent them on no-fault going forward, and notify the no-fault insurer that representation has ended.

Title: Service of Process on a Domestic Corporation in New York | Firm: Buffalo Injury Law Firm, PLLC | Effective Date: July 24, 2025 | Prepared/Approved By: Max Adler

I. Purpose

To ensure proper, lawful, and efficient service of process on domestic and authorized foreign corporations in New York, consistent with CPLR § 311, BCL §§ 304 and 306.

II. Service Method Overview

Preferred sequence: (1) Attempt Personal Service (CPLR § 311); (2) if unsuccessful/impractical, use Secretary of State Service (BCL §§ 304, 306). Personal service is stronger for litigation and default purposes.

Quick Reference:

ScenarioMethodNotes
Business has an accessible officeTry CPLR 311Strongest form of service
Business address unclear/vacantUse BCL 306Efficient backup
Defendant is dodging serviceUse BCL 306Avoids delays and gamesmanship
Entity is foreign but authorized in NYUse BCL 306Same rules apply

III. Decision Tree — Service Method Selection

Step 1: Identify Entity — Use NYS DOS Entity Search to confirm corporate status, get the service-of-process address, and check for a registered agent. If not a domestic/authorized foreign corp, use the appropriate alternate rule (BCL 307, CPLR 311-a).

Step 2: Assess for Personal Service (CPLR 311) — Do you have a known physical address where an officer, director, or managing agent can be personally served, and is the location active/accessible?

Step 3: Attempt Personal Service (CPLR 311) — Email the summons/complaint/NEF to service@wnyprocess.com (Jacque), instruct service on an officer/director/agent per CPLR 311, follow up for the affidavit of service, upload to SmartAdvocate. If service fails after reasonable effort, document attempts and proceed to Step 4.

Step 4: Use Secretary of State Service (BCL 306) — Email the summons/complaint/NEF to jteitel@lawyerservicebureau.com (Jeffrey Teitel), confirm receipt, follow up for affidavit of service, upload to SmartAdvocate. Teitel handles the $40 DOS filing fee; DOS mails documents to the corporation's registered address.

items.d: How to add an adjuster to a case.
items.content:
How to add an adjuster to a case

First read the CasePeer help article on adding adjusters, then note:

  1. CasePeer houses adjuster contacts in silos based on insurance company — you must add the correct insurance company to the case before adding an adjuster (e.g., GEICO General vs. GEICO Indemnity; Progressive Advance vs. Progressive Casualty are different entities). Adding the wrong one creates duplicate/confused contacts.
  2. Always check if the adjuster already exists in the dropdown before creating a new one — never duplicate adjusters.
  3. If the adjuster doesn't exist, gather: first/last name, gender, phone, fax, email.
There are 6 fields for insurance adjusters — call the insurance company's main claim number to confirm each: (1) Bodily Injury, (2) PIP adjuster, (3) UM/UIM/SUM adjuster, (4) Property Damage adjuster.

Filled-in example format:

Case name — Venue: County/Court/Judge.

Plaintiff: Brief description — e.g. Lauren Torrisi, age 28, currently married, no children, works as x-ray technician.

Defendants: Owner of vehicle, [Name], and [Name] as driver / Canadian citizen.

Insurance: Primary: $250,000 with Travelers; First excess: $1,000,000 CA with Desjardins.

Incident: Rear-end auto v. auto collision. Example narrative: on [date] at 4:00 p.m., plaintiff was traveling on I-90 south, stopped for heavy rush-hour traffic, when the defendant, for unknown reason, failed to stop in time and hit her from behind.

Injuries: e.g. mild brain injury, cervical herniated discs, labral tear requiring arthroscopy.

Treatment: List each provider and last visit date.

Economic Damages: Past/future loss of earnings, past/future medical bills.

Settlement: Track offers and dates, e.g. "Plaintiff served policy limits offer on [date]. [Insurer] $$$ offer on [date]."

Purpose

This SOP exists to encourage and empower the team to think critically, seek answers, and propose solutions — rather than relying on seniority as the first and only source of answers.

Step 1: Check Firm Knowledge Resources

Before asking the attorney, always look in the firm's knowledge base (this wiki / Notion). Use search to locate policies, templates, workflows, prior case notes, or FAQs — many questions are already answered there.

Step 2: Use ChatGPT as a Research Assistant

If the answer isn't in the knowledge base, use ChatGPT with a clear, focused prompt (e.g., "Summarize this expert report in plain English, highlight inconsistencies" or "Draft a response to this client inquiry/adjuster request using NY law"). Review the firm's AI Policy on uploading case-related documents first.

Step 3: Critically Evaluate the Answer

Don't accept the first answer blindly — ask: does this make sense in context? Does it align with what I already know? Is anything missing? Refine the prompt if needed.

Step 4: Prepare a Proposed Answer or Solution

Write down: the question/problem, the research/steps taken, and your proposed answer.

Step 5: Present to the Attorney

Only after Steps 1–4, bring the matter to the attorney — always with the question plus your proposed answer, ready to explain your reasoning.

Golden Rule

Never bring a question without first attempting to solve it.

You are capable of thinking critically and finding solutions — this builds skill, confidence, and independence, and strengthens the firm.

items.t: Limiting Fees for Records in E-Format
items.d: Response to medical provider attempting to charge us per page fees for records
items.content:
Task Information

Purpose: Use this to hold medical facilities accountable when they attempt to overcharge for electronic medical records.

Case Citation: Frey v. Mount Sinai Medical Center

New York County Supreme Court decision (Index #805389/2022, Hon. John J. Kelley, March 28, 2023) compelling a hospital to produce medical records electronically and rejecting the hospital's photocopy-fee argument. This case is very useful as a citation in response to a hospital's request for photocopy fees — quote or cite it.

Sample Response Letter

Dear [Hospital/Medical Provider]: We received your request for payment of photocopy fees for our client's medical records. On behalf of [client], our request is limited to electronic medical records. The maximum fee for production of electronic medical records under federal and New York law is $6.50. In Frey v. Mount Sinai Medical Center (Sup. Ct. NY Cnty, March 28, 2023), the court held that under 45 C.F.R. § 171.302(b)(2), a fee cannot be based on the electronic access of an individual's electronic health information by the individual, their personal representative, or a designated person — including the individual's attorney. This federal regulation expressly prohibits per-page fees for electronic medical records; HHS guidance permits only a $6.50 charge for first-class postage to mail a CD. Under New York law, patients (and those similarly situated, including litigation counsel) are generally entitled to court orders granting access to records for litigation purposes, whether litigation has been commenced or is merely contemplated. In Frey, the court ordered production of a complete set of electronic health information on CD/thumb drive/other accessible medium, limited to a $6.50 postage charge. We will gladly advance a check for $6.50. If you do not produce the complete electronic records, we will be forced to compel production and seek statutory costs and disbursements as part of that motion. — The Buffalo Injury Law Firm, PLLC

Task Information

Assigned to: Legal assistant | Purpose: Make sure investigation and evidence collection is followed up on and completed.

Home

Insurance Audit

For defendants and client, confirm: name of insurance companies; names/emails/phones for all adjusters; claim number; liability and coverage status; limits; LOA (letter of acknowledgment) saved in Correspondence folder. We need all insurance limits in writing with adjuster contact info.

What This Is

How to ensure we have all of a client's medical providers so we can order their records.

Why We Do It This Way

Medical records are the foundation of injury cases — without them there's no evidence of injuries, pain, and suffering, and no way to determine medical conditions/diagnoses.

How We Do This

  • Request the PIP payment log from the client's no-fault adjuster every 2-3 months; review it with the client to confirm it lists all providers seen since the crash, and add any missing ones (name/number/address) to the medical treatment tab.
  • Call the client and ask for provider name(s) and address(es).

Prior Medical History: Always ask about the client's prior medical history to assess whether they've had prior claims/injuries to the same body parts now being claimed. If yes, get records from those providers — this will delay the case if not done before trying to settle.

Quick guide on adding insurance medical examiners to a case.

Once a report comes in

items.t: SOP: Referring a Case to a New Attorney
items.content:
Task Information

Purpose: Ensure that when a case is referred to another attorney, the file is complete, organized, and sent professionally and consistently.

Procedure

  1. Confirm with the lead attorney: new attorney's name, firm, email/phone, and any special instructions (deadline, limited scope, co-counsel vs. full transfer).
  2. Confirm with the client: they agree to the referral, and their contact info (phone, email, address) is current.
  3. Create a "Referral folder" shared folder.
  4. Gather all relevant, non-privileged documents: medical records/bills, correspondence with insurers, spoliation/preservation letters, investigation materials (police reports, MV-104, incident reports), photos/videos.
  5. Do NOT include: internal attorney notes, internal email threads, unsent drafts, strategy memos or internal case-value evaluations. If unsure whether something is privileged, ask the attorney first.
  6. Combine documents into a single organized PDF.
  7. Prepare the Costs & Disbursements Summary — paste all paid costs into the email body, excluding the firm administrative fee.
  8. Email the new attorney with the client's current contact info and itemized costs/disbursements (medical record retrieval, filing fees, service of process, postage/copying, investigator/police report fees, expert/consult fees, other).
  9. Update case status to Referred-Open (Internal).

What This Is

How to counsel clients once treatment is denied pursuant to a negative IME.

Why This Is Important

Medical care and documentation are essential to overcoming the serious injury threshold. A gap in treatment — or the client stopping treatment altogether — can significantly harm the case and reduce recovery.

What Is A Negative IME?

An "Independent Medical Examination" — called an "Insurance Medical Examination" internally because it isn't truly independent; the insurance company pays doctors who often write reports saying people are not hurt and no longer need care. These exams are scheduled by the client's no-fault carrier, so clients have no choice but to attend.

A negative IME does not suspend all treatment. Adjusters commonly misrepresent this, claiming the medical claim is now closed. In truth, only that particular specialty of care is interrupted — e.g., a negative chiropractic IME only interrupts chiropractic care, not orthopedic or pain management care.

What To Do

Step 1: Get the IME report and check the area of specialty (chiro, ortho, neuro, etc.).

Step 2: Check who is treating the client in that same specialty — many treating doctors will continue care after a negative IME because they can arbitrate for payment after a denied bill.

Step 3: Call/email the doctor's office to confirm they received the negative IME report and how they wish to proceed. Scenarios: (1) they'll treat and arbitrate later; (2) they'll treat but bill health insurance instead of arbitrating; (3) they release the patient, who then needs alternative care in a different specialty ASAP.

Step 4: Confirm the client continues some form of care.

What This Is

The process to arrange a doctor-attorney meeting to discuss a client's case before a lawsuit is filed.

Why We Do This

Before suing and going to trial, confirm the doctor's opinions on key issues (causation, diagnosis, prognosis, future care, etc.).

How We Do This

  1. Make sure records are organized (med chrono).
  2. Call the doctor's office, identify the scheduler, build rapport, identify who you represent, and request a short meeting (specify how long). Ask: how far in advance should the meeting be set, and would sending payment in advance help?
  3. Lock down a date.
  4. Draft a letter to the provider confirming the appointment, addressed to the doctor c/o the scheduler, including the patient consent form and payment check if the amount is known.
  5. Follow up with the scheduler/office as needed.
Genuine friendliness, obvious desire to help the patient, and persistence with great professionalism is the key.

This is our process for preparing a case for a lawsuit.

Why We Do It This Way

The more prepared we are, the more quickly we can push the case through litigation and set up for success overcoming defense delay tactics.

How We Do This

  1. Medical Chronology — complete a med chrono on all cases before suing (a document summarizing all treatment, hyperlinked to records in chronological order). This drastically reduces time spent on the bill of particulars, can be given to treating physicians/experts, and helps evaluate the case.
  2. Draft Summons and Complaint
  3. Draft notice of commencement of e-filing for each defendant
  4. Draft notice of action to social services (Medicaid/social services clients)
  5. Draft letter to defendant with S&C (summons and complaint)
  6. Get Verified Complaint signed
  7. Cover letter to process server
  8. Draft Defense Authorizations — prepare medical authorizations to send to defense counsel as soon as they're assigned (after filing). This expedites their record review so depositions can be scheduled early.

Communication with the Judge outside the presence of defense attorneys is ex parte communication and is prohibited. All letters/conversations with the judge must include all defense attorneys. See Rules of Judicial Conduct 100.3(6).

How It's Done

  1. Check the judge's local rules.
  2. If rules are silent on instructions, email the clerk.
  3. Copy all attorneys.
  4. Write the request.

When It's Done

If writing the court about compliance issues or to discuss an issue with the judge/clerk, email the court's point of contact and CC all opposing counsel:

"Good morning, We would like to request a compliance conference with Judge X at his/her earliest convenience to address delays in the discovery process and the need to extend the scheduling order. Thank you."

(Note: this page's stored content is identical to "Communication with the Court" below — likely a duplicated/synced Notion block.)

Auto Accident Cases

🎯 Our Goal: Get to trial as quickly as possible. Respond to discovery demands on time and with complete, sufficient responses — speed and thoroughness are not mutually exclusive.

1. What Are Discovery Demands?

Formal requests from the opposing party for facts, documents, and information. In auto cases, defense typically serves its first round with their Answer: Combined/Omnibus Discovery Demands, Notice of Deposition, Request for Medicaid/Medicare Information, Demand for Bill of Particulars (BOP), Notice of Medical Examination (IME Notice).

2. Why We Respond This Way

We can't credibly demand compliance from defense if we don't comply ourselves. Timely, well-formatted responses matter for later Motion to Compel practice. Delays in discovery delay trial — and trial is the goal.

3. Deadlines

Demand TypeDeadline (General)Notes
Interrogatories / Combined Discovery20 days (Supreme Ct.)CPLR 3133 — can extend by stipulation
Bill of Particulars30 daysCPLR 3042 — verify with court rules
Request to Produce / Documents20 daysCPLR 3122 — objections or production
Notice of Medical ExaminationPer scheduling orderCoordinate with client immediately
Medicaid/Medicare DemandVariesCPLR 4545 / MSP Act requirements

Always verify deadlines against current court rules and scheduling orders. Calendar early.

4. Step-by-Step Response Process

Step 1 — Receive & Break Down the Demands: Log receipt date in CasePeer immediately; identify each demand type and split into its own PDF; calculate and calendar all deadlines.

Step 2 — Review the Complete File (Critical Step): Stop — before drafting any response, review the complete file in OneDrive and CasePeer using the Document Checklist (Section 5). Do not guess or skip this.

Step 3 — Draft the Responses: Pull the appropriate template for each demand type; use the Question/Response format for every demand; be specific and accurate (no boilerplate where a specific answer is required); note withheld documents on the Privilege Log; have the draft reviewed by the supervising attorney.

Step 4 — Compile & Bates Stamp Documents: Compile all documents into a single PDF; Bates stamp as [ClientLastName_####] (e.g., SMITH_0001), bottom center, red text; save as "P Response to RTP (Bates 1 – [Last#])".

Step 5 — Privilege Log: For any withheld document, log Bates number (if any), date, author/recipient, general description, and basis for withholding.

Step 6 — Serve & Upload: Serve responses on defense counsel by the deadline; note the date served in the case timeline.

5. Document Checklist — Auto Cases

Collision & Liability: police/accident report, DMV report, scene photos, property damage photos (all vehicles), repair estimates/invoices, towing/storage records, traffic citations, dashcam footage, surveillance footage, cell phone records (if distracted driving at issue).

Insurance & Coverage: PIP/No-Fault application (NF-2), PIP payment logs, PIP medical exams, PIP denial/exhaustion letters, verification of PIP exhaustion, defense carrier coverage letter, reservation of rights letters, Medicare/Medicaid enrollment and lien docs, workers' comp records (if applicable).

Medical Records & Treatment: ER/urgent care records, ambulance/EMS run report, all treating physician records, PT/chiro records, MRI/CT/X-ray reports and imaging, pharmacy records, mental health records (if psych injury claimed), all bills/itemized statements, signed authorizations (HIPAA, 2307), pre-existing condition records.

Lost Wages & Economic Loss: employment verification letter, pay stubs (pre/post-accident), employer disability/leave records, self-employment records (P&L, 1099s), NF-6 lost wage claim, PIP lost wage payment log/denial, life care plan/future economic loss report.

6. Response Formatting Requirements

Every response uses the Question/Response format, each demand its own numbered entry:

1. The names and addresses of any witnesses to the accident. RESPONSE: None known to plaintiff other than those listed on the police report, produced herewith.
2. Copies of all photographs taken at or near the scene. RESPONSE: Produced. See Bates SMITH_0014 through SMITH_0031.

7. Quick Reference — Common Response Language

SituationStandard Language
Document producedProduced. See Bates [X] through [Y].
Nothing responsive existsNone known to plaintiff at this time.
Document does not existNo such document exists.
Records still being obtainedPlaintiff is in the process of obtaining records. Plaintiff reserves the right to supplement this response.
Objection + partial responseObjection: [state basis]. For medical records related to conditions raised by plaintiff in this case, see Bates [X] through [Y].
Privileged document withheldObjection: attorney-client privilege / work product doctrine. See Privilege Log.
Item on police reportNone other than those identified on the police report produced herewith.

8. Final Reminders

Before sending to the attorney for review, confirm: complete file reviewed; every document category checked; Bates stamps applied/correct/red; production PDF correctly named; every withheld document logged; supervising attorney approved.

See also: Summary Judgment (linked companion page).

What This Is

Guide to preparing and drafting a discovery motion or motion for summary judgment.

Why We Do It This Way

Generally easier to read, with a higher likelihood chambers reviews everything you want reviewed — and if you have to appeal, you can copy and paste.

How We Do It

First, check the individual chambers' rules. If they require a separate statement of facts, memo of law, etc., follow that format; many of these rules were rescinded and each chambers now sets its own — if there are no rules, a "briefermation" (combined brief/affirmation) is fine (used in ~95% of motions).

For discovery motions, almost every judge has rules on what must occur before a discovery motion is ripe (meet-and-confer, discussion at the PTC, etc.) — some require a certificate of conformance confirming these steps were followed. These serve as deterrents to encourage resolution without motion practice; courts may dismiss a motion if you haven't followed them.

items.t: What to do when Answer is filed
items.content:
DO IMMEDIATELY WHEN RECEIVED

Why We Do This: We want to push litigation and take charge of the case early — being prepared and getting the defense relevant information early lets us push cases through litigation and apply pressure with settlement opportunity letters.

Step 1: Breakdown documents

Break down the combined PDF into individual files and save to the correct folder (e.g., Demand for BOP → pleadings; Demand for Medicaid/Medicare → discovery; Notice of EBT → depositions).

Step 2: Calendar deadlines

Refer to the Litigation Deadlines procedure for all deadlines.

Step 3: Annotate the Complaint

Review the Answer and highlight each allegation in the complaint by color based on denial, admission, or without-knowledge response. Save as "Complaint - annotated." Repeat for each defendant's answer. (See Sample Annotated Complaint.)

Step 4: Serve Authorizations

Serve defense authorizations for all medical providers.

Step 5: Serve Medical Records

Serve all medical records already sent with the settlement letter.

Step 6: Prep Discovery and BOP

Prepare responses and our requests.

What This Is

A checklist on how to set up a deposition of a non-party, or get documents from a non-party, in New York state court.

Determine The Type of Subpoena

  • Subpoena requiring attendance to testify (CPLR 2301, 2305(a))
  • Subpoena duces tecum to produce/inspect/copy/photograph books, papers, things (CPLR 2301, 3120(1)(i))
  • Subpoena duces tecum to permit entry onto land/property to inspect/measure/survey/sample/test/video (CPLR 3120(1)(ii))
  • Testimonial and document subpoenas may be combined or issued separately (CPLR 2305)

Determine Whether a Court Order Is Required

Required for: compelling production of Mental Hygiene Law §33.13 clinical records; production of an original where a certified copy is admissible; attendance of a prisoner; a patient's medical/clinical records absent authorization; records of a library or municipal/state department; a subpoena issued outside the court's jurisdiction; pre-action disclosure to aid bringing an action, preserve information, or aid arbitration.

Draft the Subpoena

Start from the CasePeer template "Subpoena DT to witness" (Litigation > Court Details).

Scope:

  • Disclosure Subpoena — nonparty must disclose all relevant matter if they had a cause of action/defense in the suit, are about to leave the state, are located out of state, reside 100+ miles from trial, are unlikely to attend due to illness, are a medical/dental/podiatry provider who treated the demanding party or was retained as an expert, or are given notice of the reasons disclosure is sought.
  • Trial Subpoena — limited to specific documents/testimony for trial use; should not seek info that should've been obtained in discovery.
  • Enforcement Subpoena — may seek info relevant to satisfying a money judgment.

Issuing Court: the court where the underlying action is pending (or, for CPLR 5224 enforcement subpoenas, any court where a special proceeding could be commenced).

Contents: issuing court identity, case caption, subpoena type/title, name/address of person subpoenaed, return date (≥20 days after service), place for production/testimony, contempt/penalty warning if court-ordered, issuing attorney's signature/name/address/phone/date/party represented, explanation for nonparty disclosure. Document subpoenas must describe items with reasonable particularity and note the medical-records authorization requirement. Testimonial subpoenas must designate a witness/topics for entity depositions per 22 NYCRR §202.20-d, state deposition location, and note videotaping details if applicable.

Who May Issue/Sign: a party's attorney, the clerk of court (pro se litigants), the judge, or the attorney general.

Serve the Subpoena

Who may serve: any non-party over 18.

What to serve with it: document subpoenas — extra copies for the witness and for the court; testimonial subpoenas — witness fees ($15/day, or $18/day for a deposed nonparty), mileage ($0.23/mile each way), folio fee ($0.10/100 words) if a transcript is required; information subpoenas — original + copy of written questions and a prepaid return envelope.

When to serve: trial subpoena — at issuing party's discretion with reasonable time to comply; disclosure subpoena — ≥20 days before deposition or document return date; hospital/medical record document subpoena — ≥3 days before return date; library/municipal record subpoena — ≥1 day before; enforcement subpoena — ≥10 days before; information subpoena — 7 days before. Not to be served/returnable on Sunday, or Saturday for a Sabbath-observant person.

Where to serve: anywhere in NY if issued from Supreme, County, Surrogate's, Family Court, or Court of Claims; NYC civil/district/city/justice courts are limited to that county and adjoining counties absent a court order.

How to serve: same manner as a summons (CPLR 2303, 307-312); a trial testimonial subpoena for a party may be served on the party's attorney; information subpoenas by registered/certified mail or electronically with consent.

Notify Other Parties: copy of a document subpoena served promptly after/at service on the witness; all parties get 20 days' written notice of a testimonial deposition subpoena stating time/place/deponent.

Proof of Service: case caption, subpoena type, description of person served, facts showing authorized service (affiant not a party, over 18, time/date/location/method of service), items served. Retain in case files; file with the court only if served on a partnership via delivery-and-mailing or affixing-and-mailing.

Comprehensive list of relevant litigation deadlines.
Litigation EventWhen PermittedTimeliness
Service of process—120 days after Complaint is filed
Answer—Personal service: 20 days. Mail: 30 days. Authorized agent: 40 days. Affixing to door: 40 days. Publication: 58 days.
Bill of ParticularsAny time after Answer served30 days after service
Omnibus/Combined DemandsAny time after filing Complaint20 days after service
Request to Produce Documents and ThingsAny time after filing Complaint20 days after service
Request for AdmissionsAny time after service of Answer20 days after service

This is our policy for serving release authorizations upon defense counsel.

Why We Do It This Way

Timely serving authorizations keeps the case moving and removes a common excuse for adjourning depositions ("we haven't had time to get the medical records"). Failing to serve on time can delay the case or trigger a Motion to Compel.

How We Do This

  1. Find a blank, signed HIPAA authorization in OneDrive.
  2. Complete all fields: patient name, DOB, address; provider name/address (from CasePeer Medical tab); recipient name/address (the defense firm, not the individual attorney); specific info to be released (all records/notes/bills/films/diagnostics LIMITED to the claimed body parts); reason for release ("Litigation"); expiration date ("End of litigation"); patient signature; today's date.
  3. Create a separate authorization for each facility, not each individual doctor.
For any authorization provided to a defense firm, we must have a copy of the records released to the defense. If we don't, request a copy right away.

Task Information

Assignment: Paralegal | Estimated Completion Time: 3-5 days | Purpose: Send the insurance company all settlement paperwork so the client gets paid once an agreement is reached.

Procedure

  1. Send client the release to sign and return.
  2. Draft Stipulation of Discontinuance with attorney's signature.
  3. Draft Ltr to OC - Release and Stip (Defendants tab > Legal Representation).
  4. Email defense attorney and adjuster the signed release, firm W-9, and paste the letter body into the email.
  5. Send Ltr to OC, Stipulation, Release, and W-9 via certified mail (Docsmit).

Why We Do It This Way

The defense likes to operate in ambiguity. We must narrow the issues and get all the back-end terms agreed to before agreeing to arbitrate — typically via the arbitration agreement defense counsel provides. Stay firm and professional; do not agree to arbitrate until all essential issues below are settled.

What We Must Consider Beforehand

  1. High-Low agreements
  2. Number of arbitrators
  3. Names of arbitrators
  4. Costs of arbitration and who pays
  5. Interest, costs, and disbursements
  6. Release language
  7. Issues to be litigated
  8. Evidence needed, including expert witnesses

Standard language: "There are no other material terms to the settlement other than as set forth in this mediation agreement." / "The mediation agreement sets forth the intent of the parties and shall be attached and adopted by any subsequent release."

Communication with the Judge outside the presence of defense attorneys is ex parte communication and is prohibited. All letters/conversations with the judge must include all defense attorneys. See Rules of Judicial Conduct 100.3(6).

How It's Done

  1. Check the judge's local rules.
  2. If rules are silent, email the clerk.
  3. Copy all attorneys.
  4. Write the request.

When It's Done

Email the court's point of contact and CC all opposing counsel:

"Good morning, We would like to request a compliance conference with Judge X at his/her earliest convenience to address delays in the discovery process and the need to extend the scheduling order. Thank you."

Goal: Get to Trial in 18 Months

The goal is to get every case to trial within 18 months of filing the lawsuit. Not always realistic in every venue, but achievable if the 9-step checklist deadlines are met — this does not happen automatically; the case manager must ensure each step is completed on time.

Monthly Progress Meeting: each month, the team assesses progress on every active lawsuit — what's been done over the past month and what's next — to make sure no case is neglected.

Step 1: Prepare the Prelitigation Checklist

Ask the questions in the Prelitigation Checklist, including provider names/addresses (hospitals, doctors, pharmacies) and employers for the last 10 years, witnesses to treatment/injuries, and photo/video evidence. Forward to the Litigation Team to ensure the right documents are set forth in the plaintiff's Response to Combined Demands. Why: lawsuits stall without a complete set of medical/employment records — defense counsel's most common complaint.

Step 2: Confirm Each Defendant Has Served an Answer

Check the Litigation tab to confirm each defendant served their Answer (which, with rare exception, denies the Complaint's allegations). Notify the attorney if any defendant hasn't answered. Why: if a defendant isn't served within 120 days of filing, the lawsuit is automatically dismissed.

Step 3: Request a Complete Set of Medical Records

Request a complete records set for all treatment dates from the records retrieval company via a signed HIPAA release, then serve them (with release authorizations) on defendants' attorneys. Why: incomplete medical records in defense counsel's hands is the most common cause of litigation delay.

Step 4: Serve Release Authorizations Upon Defendants' Attorneys

Two types: (1) medical records authorizations, and (2) "Arons" (speaking) authorizations allowing defense counsel to speak with treating doctors — prepare Arons authorizations ONLY if defense counsel requests them. Why: avoids delay; defense won't schedule depositions without a complete authorization set.

Step 5: Serve the Plaintiff's Verified Bill of Particulars

The BOP expands on the complaint's allegations and the injuries/damages sustained, prepared in Question-and-Answer format responding to defendant's demand. Why: the most important legal document in the lawsuit — improper theories of liability/injury/damages won't be admissible at trial.

Step 6: Serve Plaintiff's Discovery on Defendants' Attorneys

For most auto cases, the first round is 3101 demands, plaintiff's first Request to Produce, and Demand for Verified Bill of Particulars, served with the plaintiff's Response to Combined Discovery Demands. Why: without requesting medical records, statements, employment contracts, and surveillance video, we lack the evidence to prove our client's case.

Step 7: Serve Plaintiff's Combined Discovery Responses

Respond to defendants' Combined Discovery Demands (statements, medical reports, Arons authorizations, tax records, witnesses, photographs, incident reports, diaries/logs, experts, collateral source info) using CasePeer templates; call the client or ask the attorney for unknown answers. Object within 20 days to improper portions under CPLR 3122(a) (stating reasons with reasonable particularity) — common objections: tax returns, Medicare/disability files, psych records, addiction counseling records, overly broad social-media or medical-history demands. Why: cases don't progress toward trial without timely (within 20 days) combined discovery responses.

Step 8: Schedule the Depositions

Plaintiff's Deposition: contact defense counsel's deposition coordinator to schedule; confirm in writing. Defendants' Depositions: contact defense counsel for each named defendant, confirm each by letter, then immediately draft/serve the Notice of Deposition via NYSCEF. Non-Party Depositions: may be conducted any time after filing; ask the attorney whether non-party depositions are needed and who, then prepare a subpoena for a process server. Why: diligence in scheduling depositions could be the most important thing you do to move the case toward trial.

Step 9: File the Note of Issue to Request a Trial Date

The Note of Issue affirms discovery is complete and requests a trial date; file with the court (with fee) and serve via NYSCEF. Then write the judge requesting the first available trial date, CC'ing all defense attorneys. Why: a confirmed trial date is the ultimate goal — cases don't resolve without completing discovery and requesting a date.

items.t: How to respond to bill of particulars (BOP)
items.content:
Our goal is to get to trial as quickly as possible — we must respond to discovery demands on time and sufficiently, without cutting corners on documents/depositions needed to prove our case.

Omnibus Demands

Email the client the final BOP and verification for signature in front of a notary.

(See also the firm's dedicated bop-drafting skill/SOP for full response drafting across MVA, pedestrian, and slip/fall case types.)

Task Information

Assignment: Paralegal | Estimated Completion Time: Depends on scheduling order | Purpose: Required by Federal Rule 26(a)(1) to give parties an early evidentiary guide and disclosure.

This page is a blank internal template — the specific step-by-step procedure was not filled in as of the last export. Follow FRCP 26(a)(1)'s baseline requirements (disclosure of witnesses, documents, damages computation, and insurance agreements) until this SOP is completed.

• All documents in civil, criminal, miscellaneous civil, miscellaneous criminal, and new civil case openings — except sealed documents, sealed cases, criminal case openings, miscellaneous case openings, and as otherwise expressly provided — must be electronically filed on the system.
  • Emailing or faxing a document to the Clerk's Office or the assigned judge does not constitute "filing" of that document.
  • Filings must be in PDF format, less than 50 MB in size.

Introduction

Follow this guide to draft a motion for summary judgment (MSJ). Every motion consists of: (1) notice of motion, (2) statement of material facts, (3) memo of law (case law), (4) evidence in admissible form.

Notice of Motion

Must include: case caption; return date; titles of supporting papers (memo of law, affidavits/affirmations, and — for discovery/BOP disputes — an affirmation of good faith); relief sought (with grounds/statute); brief summary of the action; request for oral argument if desired/permitted; names/addresses of other parties to be served; note that under CPLR 2214(b) answering papers must be served at least 7 days before the return date; moving attorney's signature block. Check local rules and the assigned justice's individual practices for additional requirements.

Memo of Law

Cover sheet (caption, title, attorney contact); preliminary statement; summary of relevant facts; legal arguments; relief requested; signature block.

Admissible Evidence

May include affidavits, affirmations, deposition transcripts, photographs, diagrams, maps, records, reports, contracts, bills, admissions, checks, receipts, DVDs, disclosure responses/failures, stipulations, authenticated/certified documents. Affidavits must be signed/notarized; EBTs certified by the court reporter and signed by the deponent; business/medical records certified under CPLR 3122(a) or affirmed under CPLR 4518(a); photos need an affidavit authenticating what they depict.

Affidavits

What: a sworn written document attesting facts under penalty of perjury. Why: lets you piece together the case's facts in one document for the court. Practice Tips: first person; may reference attached exhibits; focus on material facts; no legal argument; drop legalese ("sworn" not "duly sworn," "states" not "deposes and says," etc.). Must include: case caption; title; state/county of signing; affiant's name/qualifications and statement of personal knowledge or basis of information/belief; facts grouped by heading, told chronologically, in admissible form.

When: when there's a good-faith basis to believe no dispute exists about a material fact (e.g., summary judgment). Timing: after issue is joined — after the defendant answers (or plaintiff replies to a counterclaim). Standard: the moving party bears the burden to establish, with facts, each element entitling it to judgment as a matter of law. Evidence: extract from pleadings, verified bill of particulars, depositions, written admissions, discovery responses; use affidavits to fill evidentiary gaps.

CPLR 3212 (summary judgment statute), key provisions:

  • (a) Time: any party may move after issue is joined; if no court-set deadline, no later than 120 days after filing the Note of Issue absent leave of court for good cause.
  • (b) Supporting proof: must be supported by affidavit (by a person with knowledge, reciting all material facts), a copy of the pleadings, and other available proof (depositions, admissions). Granted if the papers establish no triable issue as a matter of law; denied if any party shows facts requiring a trial. Court may grant judgment to a non-moving party without a cross-motion if warranted.
  • (e) Partial summary judgment/severance: may be granted as to one or more causes of action (except in favor of a non-moving party in a matrimonial action); the court may sever the resolved claim or hold entry in abeyance pending the rest.
  • (f) Facts unavailable to opposing party: court may deny the motion or grant a continuance for further affidavits/disclosure.
  • (g) Limitation of issues for trial: the court may specify undisputed/incontrovertible facts, which become established for all purposes in the action.
  • (h) Public petition and participation cases: MSJ granted unless the responding party shows a substantial basis in fact/law or a substantial argument for changing existing law; such motions get a hearing preference.
  • (i) Licensed architect/engineer/surveyor/landscape architect cases: similar heightened standard tied to Notice of Claim requirements under CPLR 214(1); hearing preference applies.

Affirmations

What: like affidavits (subject to perjury penalties) but don't require a notary. Use if: the affiant is a NY attorney, a NY physician/osteopath/dentist, or located outside the US. Why: offers evidence in admissible form (e.g., EBTs, undisputed documents) and lets litigators tell the client's story. Practice Tips: first person; numbered paragraphs, one topic per paragraph; reference exhibits; material facts only; no legal argument; drop legalese. Must include: same structural elements as affidavits (caption, title, venue, affiant qualifications, facts grouped/chronological/admissible, bookmarked exhibits, pleadings attached, RJI and addendum, proof of service, and — for discovery/BOP motions — a good-faith affirmation per 22 NYCRR §202.7(a),(c)).

Redaction requirements: all but the last 4 digits of SSN/taxpayer ID; month/day (not year) of DOB; a minor's name (initials only); all but the last 4 digits of a financial account number; material protected by Domestic Relations Law §235 or sealed matrimonial evidence used in a non-matrimonial action; student names in Education Law Article 129-B proceedings (per CPLR 3016(i)). If confidential information must be included, apply for a sealing order or leave to file a confidential affidavit/affirmation.

Color key: Green = Admission | Yellow = Without Knowledge | Red = Denial

The worked example itself is a PDF attachment (a verified, indexed Summons and Complaint) stored in the firm's Notion and was not extractable as text. Use this color-coding key when annotating any answer against the complaint, per the "What to do when Answer is filed" procedure.
